So if I understand correctly the idea is that:

```python
class SomeException(Exception):
    pass

def specific_error_handler(request: Request, exc: SomeException) -> Response:
    ...

Starlette(exception_handlers={SomeException: specific_error_handler})  # ok
Starlette(exception_handlers={Exception: specific_error_handler})  # not okay
```
?

Maybe there's something to be said for forcing a signature:

```python
ExceptionHandler = Callable[[Request, Exception], Union[Response, Awaitable[Response]]]
```

And then having users either accept the type error, use a `# type: ignore` when they define the mapping of error handlers or always write their error handlers to accept an `Exception` type and do an `isisntance` check or a `# type: ignore` in the error handler.

Personally, I would just always write my error handler to accept an `Exception` and raise an error if I got the wrong type (because I likely configured my app incorrectly).

Sorry this took so long to get to, @henryiii, but I managed to overcome my fear and give this another look ;-)

I'm still a not very comfortable with the whole rabbit hole of packaging, but I can't really shake the feeling that there should be an easier way than all the hoops you are now jumping through. However: 1. that might just be because I'm missing a few details or problems you encountered meanwhile, and 2. I think I've reconciled myself with the overall architecture. So as far as I'm concerned, this is good, and we can learn and adapt later, if necessary.

Two more things:
- I'm not a fan of how much it costs in complexity just to avoid repeating the version number. Can't we rather add a test that checks whether `_version.py` and `version.h` agree? 
_(I will admit I have a line like `version_match = re.search(r"^#define PARSELMOUTH_VERSION ([0-9a-z.]+)$", version_file, re.M)` in my `setup.py`, but that somehow does seem a lot simpler. It's actually mostly the filling-in of template `.in` files and copying them around that's confusing to me :-/ )_
Alternatively: is it a lot of effort to move this to another PR, to be looked at separately there? Probably it is, actually, so ... yeah, that probably doesn't make sense either :-/
- Do we really need to have a `setup.py` and sdist that's easily installable? Isn't that what wheels are for, to be readily-installable, without building from source, so without needing a bunch of development tools like a modern CMake, etc? If I understand, a lot of the copying-around and having-multiple-setup.py-files and other complexity originates here, or is that not true?

This all being said, I'm fine to have this go in and actually test it out in the real world! So I'm not blocking on any of these issues. We can always change things later, if necessary.
Don't take my approval too heavily either, though; I'm not an expert on packaging ;-) A few more pair of eyes and reviews would be useful (but maybe not too likely to happen? :-( ).

I still haven't checked the problem in depth, but maybe it's because we're using `retry_range` to both try first time and start retrying? With an example:

```python
# good, we need a first attempt to connect but 0 retries means we won't retry connecting
for attempt in retry_range(retries):
    connect()

# bad, we don't want to try reconnecting but 0 will still try once
for attempt in retry_range(retries):
    reconnect()
```

I definitely got lazy with all the red CI emails numbing me there. So this is `PEX_EXTRA_SYS_PATH` propagation I added in Pex 2.1.95. The `./build-support/bin/release.sh build-wheels` uses `./pants run ...` under the covers and that tacks on path items using `PEX_EXTRA_SYS_PATH`. In particular it tacks on just the things the `build-support/bin/_release_helper.py` script needs, which includes a subset of `src/python/pants/util`. Although `pants` is a namespace package, `pants.util` is not; so boom. So this concept of propagating `PEX_EXTRA_SYS_PATH` falls down for non-ns packages in the right circumstances. I'll be able to think about how / where this should be fixed more clearly in the am, but a revert is definitely the right thing to do here now.

I tried the branch and get multiple errors during compilation on my Mac:
> error: ambiguous partial specializations of 'type_caster<const int>' const_name<Const>("FrozenSet[", "Set[") + key_conv::name

Thus I was unable to test it further, however I have some observations.

1. Personally I do not support an idea of the implicit cast from `non-const std::set` to `frozenset`. As I mentioned in https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/discussions/3836 -- there should be a clear line between `set` and it's immutable version as it might confuse Python/pybind users. Ideally functions should only accept dicts coming (or getting out) in form of `std::map<const std::set<...>, ...>`. Is there a way to restrict that?
2. I have also noticed that tests lack some of essential scenarios. What happens in case when code mix const and non-cost types? What happens when arguments are passed by copy?
3. Following @Skylion007 concern about tuples as I have similar thoughts. `std::tuple` is already filling that niche, there is no need to expand in that area. As Python tuple will be converted to `std::vector` it will not only allow appending, but actually allow to modify it's contents -- I do not think this is an expected behaviour. If a user wants to do it, just use `py::tuple` and manually transfer the data to the mutable container.

```python
for line in io.StringIO(_params_str):
    if line.startswith('[boosting: '):
        self.params['boosting'] = line.strip().replace(f"[boosting: ", "").replace("]", "")
```

and could instead just pass the result of `LGBM_BoosterGetConfig()` to a JSON parser like `json` in Python or `{jsonlite}` in R.

For the test, I think a good test would be to generate an image that creates all possible flow values. Something like this:

```py
import torch
from torchvision.utils import flow_to_image
from torchvision.io import write_jpeg
h, w = 100, 100

flow = torch.meshgrid(torch.arange(h), torch.arange(w), indexing="ij")
flow = torch.stack(flow[::-1], dim=0).float()
flow[0] -= h/2
flow[1] -= w/2

img = flow_to_image(flow)
torch.save(img, "expected_flow.pt")
write_jpeg(img, "lol.jpg")  # if you want to look at it
```

If I understand correctly, the approach here assumes that the asset partitions on an upstream output are always the same as the asset partitions on the input. Unfortunately I don't think it's always safe to assume that.

Consider this situation:
- Assets A and B are both daily-partitioned. B depends on A.
- There's a "rolling window" partition dependency. I.e. each partition of asset B depends on the last 3 partitions of asset A.  E.g. "2021-04-06" depends on "2021-04-06", "2021-04-05", and "2021-04-04".

In this case, `input_context.upstream_output.asset_partition_key_range` might be ("2021-04-06", "2021-04-06"), while `input_context.asset_partition_key_range` would be ("2021-04-04", "2021-04-06").

This makes me think that the best way to fix the code example that you linked might be to fix the implementation of `fs_asset_io_manager` to use `input_context.asset_partition_key` instead of `input_context.upstream_output.asset_partition_key`.

1. Cluster task manager can accept tasks, but it will always reject to schedule on a local node (test 1)

3. When a task is "already queued", we should cover all state transitions 
    5. scheduling is waiting for pulling objects and failed -> drain -> task is rejected or canceled (test 3) 
    6. scheudling is waiting for workers and failed -> drain -> task is rejected or canceled (test 4)
    7. scheduling is waiting for runtime env and failed -> drain -> task is rejected or canceled (test 5)
    8. scheduling succeeds -> drain (test 6)

Thanks for starting to break this into smaller pieces! I only had a few minutes to quickly scan this, so left some minor comments. I'll leave a more thorough review when I have time.

Could you please describe here why the approach you're recommending is to use `pyarrow` + converting in that library to corresponding C API functions, instead of having LightGBM link to `libarrow` and using its entrypoints directly?

Linking to `libarrow` might complicate LightGBM's build process and it'd introduce some conversations about how we want to handle packaging, but it'd also mean that Arrow support would also be available to projects integrating directly with LightGBM's C API. It'd also reduce the amount of additional effort needed to support this in the R package, for example.

I'm NOT saying that we definitely should take that approach, I just would like to hear why you chose not to involve linking to `libarrow` here.

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants run :runner`:

| action | should restart `:runner`? | actually restart? | pass |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. editing `runner.py` | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark: | 
| 2. editing `dep.py` | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark:  |
| 3. editing the comment in `generator.py` (no output change) | :x: | :x: (`pants.log` implies the generator is rerun) | :white_check_mark:  |
| 4. editing the print in `generator.py` (output changes) | :white_check_mark: | :white_check_mark: | :white_check_mark: 
| 5. editing `unrelated.py` | :x: | :x:  | :white_check_mark: |
| 6. editing `:runner` in `BUILD` (e.g. adding tags) | :white_check_mark:* | :white_check_mark: | :white_check_mark: |
| 7. editing other parts of `BUILD`, e.g. comments | :x: | :x: | :white_check_mark: |

\* For the specific case of editing tags, I don't think this could ever change the runtime behaviour(?), so arguably no restart would be better behaviour... but that seems like a detail specific to only a few fields, and doesn't apply to all situations (e.g. `--filter-tag-regex` target filtering means tag changes can change the behaviour for other goals, as tested below).

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants test ::`, and while it is running:

| action | should restart? | actually restart? | pass |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8. edit `test_fast.py` | only `test_fast.py` | both `test_fast.py` and `test_slow.py` | :x: |
| 9. edit `test_slow.py` | only `test_slow.py` | both `test_fast.py` and `test_slow.py` | :x: |

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants test --filter-tag-regex=some_tag ::`, and while it is running:

| action | expected behaviour | actual behaviour | pass |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10. add `some_tag` to `test_fast.py` | `test_fast.py` is run, `test_slow.py` continues running | `test_fast.py` is run (`test_slow.py` also restarts, covered by 8) | :white_check_mark: |
| 11. remove `some_tag` from `test_slow.py` | `test_slow.py` is interrupted and the test run edits immediately | as expected | :white_check_mark: |

Hi @Kirmas and sorry for the delay. I had some time to look into this, and I think we should split this PR to make it easier to manage, review and test:
* First PR should just involve adding the spec files & add necessary properties for accessing the device properties on the main device. This PR should also restructure the codebase to create a new `yeelight` package that will host all the files relevant to the yeelight "integration".
* Second PR would be adding the support for color flows. Although this could perhaps be merged with the first one, if it looks that it doesn't require many changes.
* Third PR would concern only about extending the integration to support night light / background light (assuming those are separate?). This will involve some thinking about the API design, but I think the sublights should implement the same interface as the main one while being backed by the data from the main light. The interfaces would check for the prerequisites (e.g., if trying to perform a non-supported call) and handle the query building (i.e., handling the method call prefixing).

I still hold the same opinion as I shared a year ago - on renaming the _OperationContext_ to _SecurityContext_ (or) _UserContext_ (or) _AuthContext_. If we take a look at the current _OperationContext_, all it has is user and client details. It does not hold any information about the actual operation. > Reviewed 9 of 37 files at r1. > *[Reviewable](https://reviewable.io/reviews/vmware/container-service-extension/1065#-:-McAlxGr8Q8gMa-w3ENr:bg7igkv)* status: 9 of 37 files reviewed, all discussions resolved (waiting on @sahithi)

This feature is awesome! I really like it, not only because it will make things easier for me personally, but also we could invite people to upload their data to Zenodo or figshare by promoting an easy way to download it with Pooch.

The only thing I don't like of the implementation is the need to set `figshare` or `zenodo` as the _protocol_ of the url: I think it could be really confusing having them in the same set of `http` or `ftp`, while they are just strings that are being passed to tell the downloader which API should be used to fetch the desired files. I wouldn't add a separate argument for them neither.

But, what if we grab the repository out of the `doi`? As far as I know, we can use `urllib` to open any `https://doi.org/xxxx/xxxxx` url and find the original source:

```python
import urllib

url = "https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.13604360"
urllib.parse.urlparse(urllib.request.urlopen(url).geturl()).netloc
```
Returns:
```
'figshare.com'
```

This way we could avoid the need to pass `figshare` or `zenodo` to the url and also make easier to add a downloader for any other doi archives in the future: we would just need to extend the supported archives without the need to make a lot of changes on the documentation.

What do you think?

I've reviewed it, and I agree that this PR is as correct as I can make it out to be. My mental notes on backwards compatibility:
- If the kernel is new while the frontend is old, the kernel will keep the same behavior for "update" messages. As long as the frontend successfully discards the unknown "echo_update" message without failing (and our code does this), then everything will be as expected.
- If the kernel is old, while the frontend is new, the frontend will build up a finitely sized collections of attribute keys to message ids that it is looking for, but no performance hit or change in behavior.

As such, the main point is then about correct behavior, which is compatible with the RC phase. I do have some points of improvements related to that:

- Ideally in this PR, but can be done in follow up in RC phase: We should add one or more tests for the behavior when we set the env var to use / not use echos. Currently this is not covered by the tests?
- Follow up PR: We should add tests for the JS side of echos. Currently there are no tests checking this, and I think I could simplify the JS code for echo handling some more, but it is hard to be certain since non of the expected behavior is encoded in tests.
- Follow up PR: Can we add a note in the docstring of `send_sync_message` about what the expected return value is? E.g. "If a message is sent successfully, this returns the message ID of that message. Otherwise it returns an empty string".

Thanks @bukepo for this. At first I was supportive of adding this, but the more I think on this, the more I feel the use of `&&` may not be well-suited for indicating ownership transfer:
- Passing a `&&` to a function/method, indicates to it that the object is an R-value
- An R-value typically indicates that the reference is to a temporary object (which will be gone).
- It hints to the method/function that if it wants to copy the object, it can reuse and take over the resources/content of the passed object reference (it does not need to copy its content)
- So passing a `&&` to a function/method does not necessarily indicate that caller is passing the ownership.

Smart pointer and/or smart reference are better indicator of ownership. As I mentioned in our internal sync I have a branch to add the notion of `OwnerPtr` to use with `Message` ([branch](https://github.com/abtink/openthread/commit/2fa7c56c62e5c4758e6d2e929d11738ddca50c50)). It is on hold due to its code size overhead.

--------------

With regards to `Message` itself, one idea is to use `Message &` vs `const Message &` to indicate whether the message ownership is being passed to the method/function.
- I liked this model, but if I recall correctly, there was a challenge with it (requires some additional changes)
- I think in some functions/methods we pass a `Message &` even though the method only reads the content of the message (example of this is `Lowpan::Compress()` methods). 
- The reason for passing `Message &` seems to be that the method wants to `SetOffset()` on the message. 
- If we somehow decouple the changing of offset from the `Message` I think we may be able to use `Message &` and `const Message &` to indicate ownership transfer.

Hello Ian,
thanks for your emphasize an issue with failed tests. I really apologize if the logic of these tests is a bit implicit. But these are simulation tests for checking the consensus. A random seed is selected with each new launch. That is, if you just restart a test, then you ignore a possible critical issue in the consensus logic (or view change in this case). Could you please write down the seed on which the test falls in the list of exceptions if you do not have time to figure out what is wrong? 
I suggest not to put into the master branch rule to ignore tests that can find real bugs in the code.
As far as I understand it's a start of creating simulation tests: 
https://github.com/hyperledger/indy-plenum/pull/1242
https://github.com/hyperledger/indy-plenum/pull/1246
It's a random article  about seeds in simulation or randomization testing: https://sciprincess.wordpress.com/2019/03/14/how-to-select-a-seed-for-simulation-or-randomization/
Tomorrow I'll ask an author of these tests to get a better link to describing this approach.
UPD: The link to article from our expert: https://alexwlchan.net/2016/06/hypothesis-intro/

Thanks for the update @adetsi.  This implementation is better but the failing tests indicate another issue, that `median` can't be calculated for `integer` columns without changing the aggregate column's output type to `float`; trying to calculate the median without this will generate all 0's.  In order to do this, you'd need to set `t_tscalar median_average` explicitly, as well as setting the associated metadata for the schema transition in a few other places around the codebase. (this is done already for e.g. `count` which goes from any type to `integer`, if you need an example to grep for).

In lieu of this, it would be easier to just implement this behavior for `float` columns instead of _numeric_ ones, by changing the feature-check from `is_numeric` to `is_floating_point`.  `integer` columns will then stay `integer` when applied `median()` and exhibit the old behavior, while columns which are float will use the new behavior. This would be an acceptable alternative if the former implementation for `integer`-column-type-upgrading is too complex (it may involve updating python and the UI as well).

See inline review notes below, there are also a few small style and efficiency improvements, and we should preserve the current test inputs and their integer columns.

I am fine with the change with one caveat that we need to ensure that we users are aware of that the order that actions are executed in will change the result if the actions invoke the RNG.  For example,

```
SetRandomSeed
RandomizeMaterials
RandomizeMaterials
```

vs

```
SetRandomSeed
RandomizeMaterials
SomeOtherRandomAction
RandomizeMaterials
```

The latter will result in a different set of random materials.  This behavior is not different from say using Python's random and explicitly setting the seed.  There is also the issue of an action on the Unity side indirectly using the RNG through some other API that we call, which will affect downstream calls to the RNG.  This only matters when someone is say generating a dataset and then adds an additional call to an action within a sequence of other actions that use the RNG.

I'm not a huge fan of defining these TypedDicts in `if TYPE_CHECKING` block, but it is currently indeed necessary as they;re 3.8+, and mcstatus still supports 3.7. 

However doing this then leads to using type ignores when actually passing these dicts (although we could also use casts like `cast("RawResponsePlayer", {name="foo", id="bar"})`, which I would personally prefer). Considering the goal of this PR is to avoid type needing casts, this isn't ideal.

The way we would normally do this is to create instances of typed dicts directly, like: `RawResponsePlayer(name="foo", id="bar")`, which gives us `dict` objects on runtime, but obviously this is impossible considering we can't use these classes on runtime.

But there is a way to get around this nicely, and still essentially allow creation of these typed dicts on runtime, avoiding the need for casting (or type-ignores) completely. To do this, we can use logic like this:
```py
if TYPE_CHECKING:
    class Foo(TypedDict):
        ...
else:
    Foo = dict
    
x = Foo(...)
assert type(x) is dict  # x is simply a dict on runtime (same as initializing a typed-dict in 3.8)
typing_extensions.reveal_type(x)  # x is of type Foo (TypedDict) - evaluated only at typing-time
```

That said, I also wouldn't necessarily be completely against simply dropping 3.7 entirely, as most distributions don't ship it anymore (certainly not the bigger ones) and it is fairly outdated by now (it was released in 2018, and is already in only security fix phase, meaning no more bugs will be fixed and only major security issues will be addressed. The final end-of-life for 3.7 is still relatively far off though - 2023-06, more in [PEP 537](https://peps.python.org/pep-0537/)). But obviously, this would require another PR.
